text.title: User Control and Freedom Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every user-control-freedom application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: For reversible actions, prefer undo (toast + 5-10s window) over confirmation dialogs. Confirmation is reserved for irreversible, high-stakes operations.
rule.rationale: Confirmation overuse causes dialog fatigue; users dismiss without reading, defeating the safety purpose.

rule.statement: Content deletion defaults to soft delete (trash / archive). Permanent delete requires an explicit secondary confirmation.
rule.rationale: Soft delete preserves recovery while clearly differentiating routine from final deletion.

rule.statement: Every modal MUST have THREE exit paths: X button, Escape key, click-outside-modal. Missing any one is a violation.
rule.rationale: Different users reach for different exits; missing any path creates a trap for that population.

rule.statement: Multi-step wizards MUST show step count and provide Back navigation on every step except the first; a persistent Exit option MUST be available throughout.
rule.rationale: Trapped-in-wizard is one of the highest-friction patterns; visible escape is the fix.

rule.statement: "Are you sure?" confirmations are FORBIDDEN for non-destructive or routine actions.
rule.rationale: Routine confirmations train users to dismiss; the dialog stops working when it matters.

rule.statement: Focus traps MUST be verified with axe-core / pa11y / Playwright. Agent audit identifies structural absence; live verification is required for sign-off.

rationale.source: WCAG 2.2 — Focus Order; F-066 wave grounding

rule.rationale: Static review cannot detect runtime focus traps; tooling is mandatory.

rule.statement: An irreversible action with NO confirmation AND NO undo is severity 1 (blocker).
rule.rationale: No recovery path is the highest-cost UX defect; it blocks user trust.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
